past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 12 years ago, managed with amlodipine
Osteoporosis, diagnosed 5 years ago, on calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Hypothyroidism, diagnosed 8 years ago, well-controlled on levothyroxine
Recurrent falls over the past 18 months — 8 falls total, 3 requiring emergency department visits for evaluation (no fractures)

medications.indication: Parkinsonism (started by PCP 3 months ago, no clear improvement)

family_history: Father died at age 78 of lung cancer, no neurological disease
Mother died at age 82 of cardiac disease, no neurological disease
One brother, age 69, has type 2 diabetes
No known family history of Parkinson's disease, dementia, or other neurodegenerative disease

social_history.occupation: Retired university professor of comparative literature; retired 4 years ago. Husband notes she was still intellectually active until approximately 18 months ago when personality changes began.
social_history.living_situation: Lives with her husband in a two-story home. One adult son lives nearby.
social_history.alcohol: Rare, 1-2 glasses of wine per month
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 2 miles daily with her husband; stopped 12 months ago after multiple backward falls

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and situation. Oriented to month and year but not exact date (3 days off). MMSE 25/30 — lost 2 points on serial sevens (3 errors), lost 1 point on recall (2/3 words, retrieved third with category cue), lost 1 point on sentence writing (wrote only 3 words), lost 1 point on figure copy. MoCA 20/30 — deficits in trail-making (0/1), verbal fluency (3 F-words in 60 seconds), abstraction (0/2), delayed recall (2/5, 1 with cue), and clock drawing (2/3). Speech is slow, harsh, and strained (spastic dysarthria — different from the hypophonic speech of PD). Personality change noted by husband — more irritable, impatient, less empathetic, occasional impulsive responses. Applause sign positive (clapped 8 times, unable to stop at 3). Motor impersistence on tongue protrusion (could not maintain for 15 seconds).
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light. EXTRAOCULAR MOVEMENTS: Square-wave jerks present on primary gaze. Voluntary vertical gaze severely restricted — DOWNGAZE limited to approximately 10 degrees, UPGAZE limited to approximately 15 degrees. Horizontal saccades are slow but achieve full range. OCULOCEPHALIC REFLEX (doll's eyes): Full vertical range of eye movement is preserved when the head is passively rotated — confirms the gaze palsy is SUPRANUCLEAR (brainstem nuclei and pathways intact). Blepharospasm present. Facial expression: 'procerus sign' — deeply furrowed brow giving a characteristic worried/astonished expression. Hearing intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically but patient coughs when drinking water during exam (suggesting mild dysphagia). Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Normal bulk throughout. Tone: prominent AXIAL rigidity (neck and trunk) with relatively mild appendicular rigidity at wrists. Retrocollis present (neck held in extension — opposite of PD's anterocollis). Bradykinesia present bilaterally, approximately symmetric, on finger tapping and hand opening-closing. NO resting tremor observed during 20-minute observation. NO action tremor. Strength 5/5 throughout all extremities. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Vibration sense intact at great toes bilaterally. Proprioception intact. No cortical sensory deficits (no alien limb, no asymmetric apraxia, no cortical sensory loss).
neurological_exam.reflexes: 3+ at patellar bilaterally, 2+ elsewhere. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. Palmomental reflex present bilaterally. Grasp reflexes present bilaterally. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin mildly slow bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements moderately slowed bilaterally, symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: ERECT posture (NOT stooped — distinct from PD). Gait is slow and stiff-legged with wide base. Reduced stride length. Arms held rigidly at sides with minimal swing. Pronounced postural instability on pull test — falls BACKWARD without any compensatory stepping (examiner must catch her). Turns require 6-8 steps, en bloc. No freezing of gait. Cannot perform tandem gait.
neurological_exam.additional: Posture is notably ERECT with retrocollis (neck extension) — opposite of PD's flexed posture. No orthostatic hypotension (BP supine 148/88, standing 142/84). No inspiratory stridor. Levodopa trial: patient has been on carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 TID for 3 months. Husband reports no noticeable improvement. PCP subsequently increased to 25/250 TID (600 mg/day levodopa) for an additional 4 weeks — still no clear improvement.

patient.chief_complaint: Frequent falls and difficulty looking down
patient.history_present_illness: Dr. Priya Sharma is a 72-year-old right-handed retired university professor of comparative literature who is referred to the neurology movement disorders clinic by her primary care physician for evaluation of 'atypical Parkinson's disease' after failing to respond to levodopa therapy. Her husband, who accompanies her, provides a detailed history. Approximately 18 months ago, she began having recurrent falls. The falls have been predominantly BACKWARD — she falls straight back when standing from a chair, when turning around, or when bumped slightly from the front. She has had 8 falls in total, 3 of which required emergency department evaluation. Fortunately, she has not sustained fractures, but she did require stitches for a scalp laceration from one backward fall. Her PCP initially attributed the falls to orthostatic hypotension related to her amlodipine, but orthostatic testing was negative. Around the same time, she began having difficulty looking down. She cannot see the food on her plate without tilting her entire head down, has great difficulty navigating stairs because she cannot look at her feet, and struggles to read a book in her lap. Her husband notes that her eyes seem 'stuck' and she has to move her whole head to look at things rather than moving her eyes. Her speech has become progressively slow, harsh, and strained over the past year — different from the quiet voice she has heard in friends with Parkinson's disease. She has had increasing difficulty swallowing thin liquids, with frequent coughing and choking when drinking water or coffee. Her husband has noticed personality changes — she has become more irritable, less empathetic (she showed no emotional response when their grandson was hospitalized), and more impulsive (interrupts conversations, makes rude comments). She has never had any tremor. Her PCP started carbidopa-levodopa 25/100 TID three months ago with no improvement. The dose was increased to 25/250 TID (600 mg/day levodopa total) for the past 4 weeks, again with no clear benefit. The lack of response prompted referral to neurology. She denies visual hallucinations, urinary incontinence, or significant memory complaints (though her husband notes she sometimes forgets recent conversations).

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 8 Hz alpha activity over occipital regions, at lower limit of normal
background.anterior_posterior_gradient: Mildly disrupted with increased frontal theta

findings.description: Intermittent bifrontal theta slowing (5-7 Hz), mild and nonspecific
findings.clinical_correlation: Non-specific frontal slowing; consistent with frontal-subcortical dysfunction but does not narrow the differential.

eeg.impression: Mildly abnormal EEG showing intermittent bifrontal theta slowing. No epileptiform discharges. No electrographic seizures. The frontal slowing is nonspecific and can be seen in various conditions involving frontal-subcortical circuits.
recommended_actions: EEG is nonspecific in this context; clinical and imaging correlation is more informative

signal_characteristics.T1_sagittal: Midbrain atrophy with 'hummingbird sign' (penguin sign) on midsagittal view — the superior midbrain (tegmentum) is selectively atrophied while the pons is relatively preserved, creating the characteristic silhouette
signal_characteristics.T1_axial: 'Morning glory sign' on axial view — concavity of the lateral midbrain margin due to selective tegmental atrophy
signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Mild periventricular white matter hyperintensities, Fazekas grade 1

signal_characteristics.T1: Pontine volume relatively preserved. No hot cross bun sign. Cerebellar volume within normal limits.
Mild frontal cortical atrophy, mildly beyond expected for age
Hippocampal volumes within normal limits. Putaminal volumes within normal limits. No putaminal rim sign.
signal_characteristics.SWI: No abnormal iron deposition in putamina

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No ventriculomegaly out of proportion to sulcal atrophy
No putaminal changes to suggest MSA
No hot cross bun sign to suggest MSA-C
Third ventricle mildly enlarged, reflecting midbrain atrophy
mri.impression: Brain MRI demonstrates selective midbrain atrophy with the 'hummingbird sign' on sagittal imaging and 'morning glory sign' on axial imaging. The midbrain-to-pons ratio is reduced at 0.47 (normal >0.52). The pons, cerebellum, putamina, and hippocampi are preserved. Mild frontal atrophy is present. This imaging pattern is highly characteristic of progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), specifically the Richardson syndrome phenotype. The absence of putaminal changes or hot cross bun sign argues against MSA. The absence of hippocampal atrophy argues against AD.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High — hummingbird sign and reduced midbrain:pons ratio
Low — midbrain atrophy is not a feature of PD

recommended_actions: Correlate with clinical features including vertical gaze palsy and fall pattern
Consider DaTscan to confirm dopaminergic deficit
Assess swallowing function given reported dysphagia

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. Normal intervals. No conduction abnormalities. No ST-T wave changes.

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Within normal limits on levothyroxine replacement

labs.interpretation: Laboratory workup is entirely unremarkable. CBC and BMP are within normal limits. Thyroid function is well-controlled on levothyroxine. Vitamin B12 is adequate. Ceruloplasmin is normal, excluding Wilson disease. RPR is non-reactive. No reversible or metabolic cause for the patient's parkinsonism or cognitive symptoms is identified.

signal_characteristics.pattern: Bilateral SYMMETRIC reduction in putaminal and caudate dopamine transporter uptake

additional_observations: Bilateral symmetric reduction in striatal DAT uptake
The SYMMETRIC pattern is notably different from typical PD, which usually shows asymmetric putaminal loss
DaTscan is abnormal in PSP, PD, MSA, and DLB — it confirms dopaminergic deficit but does not distinguish between these conditions
The symmetric pattern is more consistent with PSP or MSA than with typical PD
output.impression: ABNORMAL DaTscan demonstrating bilateral symmetric reduction in dopamine transporter uptake, more severely affecting the putamina than the caudates. The symmetric bilateral pattern is atypical for idiopathic Parkinson disease, which typically shows asymmetric putaminal loss. This symmetric pattern is more consistent with an atypical parkinsonian syndrome such as PSP or MSA. DaTscan confirms presynaptic dopaminergic deficit but cannot distinguish between different causes of neurodegenerative parkinsonism.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: High (when combined with midbrain atrophy on MRI)
Low — no pontine/cerebellar findings on MRI

recommended_actions: DaTscan does not distinguish PSP from PD — clinical features and structural imaging are required
Correlate with vertical gaze palsy, fall pattern, and levodopa response

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.clinical_significance: Moderate parkinsonian motor impairment at baseline

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.value: Carbidopa 75 mg / Levodopa 300 mg + Domperidone 20 mg

Levodopa_Challenge_Test.clinical_significance: Only 8.3% improvement — well below the 30% threshold for clinically meaningful response
Only 11.1% improvement at peak — confirms poor levodopa response
Poor levodopa response is expected in PSP and should prompt reconsideration of the PD diagnosis

output.interpretation: Acute suprathreshold levodopa challenge demonstrates a poor motor response with only 11.1% improvement in UPDRS-III motor score at 90 minutes. This is well below the >30% threshold that supports a diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson disease. The poor levodopa response, combined with vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, early backward falls, axial-predominant rigidity, and midbrain atrophy on MRI, is characteristic of progressive supranuclear palsy. In PSP, the dopaminergic system is affected at multiple levels (not only nigrostriatal), explaining the poor response to levodopa replacement.

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Severely reduced phonemic fluency — one of the most sensitive measures of frontal-executive dysfunction in PSP
Significant frontal-executive dysfunction; deficits on conceptualization, lexical fluency, motor programming (Luria), and inhibitory control (go/no-go)
Episodic memory PRESERVED — an important finding distinguishing PSP from AD

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals a frontal-subcortical dementia profile with severe executive dysfunction (phonemic fluency 8 words, Trail B discontinued, Stroop z = -2.8, FAB 8/18) and moderate apathy. Critically, episodic memory (CVLT delayed recall 11/16), visuospatial function (clock drawing 8/10, Rey figure 45th percentile), and language (BNT 55/60) are all preserved. This cognitive profile — dominated by executive dysfunction with preserved memory and visuospatial function — is characteristic of PSP and reflects the frontal-subcortical circuit disruption caused by the 4-repeat tauopathy. This pattern differs from AD (where memory is typically the most impaired domain) and from DLB (where visuospatial function is prominently impaired).

Modified_Barium_Swallow_Study.value: Impaired — residue in valleculae and pyriform sinuses after swallow
SILENT ASPIRATION detected on thin liquids
Modified_Barium_Swallow_Study.clinical_significance: Silent aspiration on thin liquids — patient did not cough during aspiration events, indicating impaired laryngeal sensation
Score of 7 indicates material enters the airway, passes below the vocal folds, and no effort is made to eject (silent aspiration)

output.interpretation: Modified barium swallow study reveals moderate pharyngeal dysphagia with silent aspiration on thin liquids. There is delayed pharyngeal swallow trigger, impaired pharyngeal contraction with vallecular and pyriform sinus residue, and silent aspiration (Penetration-Aspiration Scale score 7). Aspiration is not observed with thicker consistencies. Dysphagia in PSP is common, progressive, and a major cause of morbidity (aspiration pneumonia). Recommendations: (1) Thicken all thin liquids to nectar consistency, (2) Chin-tuck posture during swallowing, (3) Speech-language pathology referral for swallowing therapy, (4) Monitor for signs of aspiration pneumonia, (5) Advance care planning discussion regarding future feeding options.
abnormal_values_summary: Silent aspiration on thin liquids (PAS score 7)

findings.location: Basal ganglia, subthalamic nucleus, and midbrain

signal_characteristics.Tau_PET: Elevated tau tracer (18F-PI-2620) binding in bilateral basal ganglia (globus pallidus > putamen), subthalamic nuclei, midbrain tegmentum, and frontal cortex
signal_characteristics.Pattern: Subcortical-predominant tau accumulation in basal ganglia and brainstem with frontal cortical extension — consistent with 4-repeat tauopathy (PSP pattern)

additional_observations: The subcortical tau distribution (basal ganglia, subthalamic nucleus, midbrain) is characteristic of 4-repeat tauopathy as seen in PSP
This pattern is DISTINCT from the cortical 3R+4R tau pattern of Alzheimer disease
Frontal cortical tau binding is mild and likely reflects secondary cortical involvement
No significant temporal or parietal tau binding (arguing against AD)
output.impression: Tau PET demonstrates elevated tracer binding in the basal ganglia, subthalamic nuclei, and midbrain, with mild frontal cortical extension. This subcortical-predominant pattern of tau accumulation is consistent with a 4-repeat tauopathy, specifically progressive supranuclear palsy. The pattern is distinct from Alzheimer disease, which shows cortical 3R+4R tau deposition primarily in temporal and parietal cortex. This finding provides molecular evidence supporting the clinical and structural imaging diagnosis of PSP.

differential_by_imaging.likelihood: Very low — no cortical temporal/parietal tau deposition
Low — CBD typically shows more asymmetric cortical tau

recommended_actions: Integrate tau PET with clinical features and structural MRI for definitive PSP diagnosis
Tau PET is emerging as a valuable biomarker for PSP but is not yet standard clinical practice

differential.key_distinguishing: Vertical gaze palsy, axial > limb rigidity, erect posture with retrocollis, early backward falls, no tremor, and poor levodopa response — all atypical for PD
No asymmetric limb apraxia, no alien limb phenomenon, no cortical sensory loss — PSP-Richardson syndrome is symmetric and axial-predominant
No prominent autonomic failure, no cerebellar signs, no pontine/cerebellar atrophy on MRI, vertical gaze palsy is rare in MSA
No visual hallucinations, no fluctuating cognition, no RBD — vertical supranuclear gaze palsy points definitively to PSP

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed eye movement examination including voluntary vertical gaze (especially downgaze), horizontal saccades, and oculocephalic reflexes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (downgaze > upgaze), slow horizontal saccades, preserved oculocephalic reflexes confirming supranuclear localization

optimal_actions.action: Characterize the fall pattern and postural instability — ask specifically about direction and timing
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Early backward falls within first 18 months — a red flag for PSP

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI assessing midbrain morphology — look for hummingbird/morning glory signs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Midbrain atrophy with hummingbird sign, reduced midbrain-to-pons ratio

optimal_actions.action: Order basic laboratory workup to exclude reversible causes
Document poor levodopa response at adequate doses
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Poor response (<30% UPDRS improvement) at suprathreshold dose

optimal_actions.action: Assess swallowing function given reported dysphagia

optimal_actions.expected_finding: Remove unnecessary medication that provides no benefit

optimal_actions.action: Fall prevention assessment and physical therapy referral

critical_actions: Assess vertical eye movements and oculocephalic reflexes to detect supranuclear gaze palsy
Document poor levodopa response at adequate doses
Order brain MRI assessing midbrain atrophy (hummingbird/morning glory signs)
Assess swallowing function given dysphagia risk
Fall prevention assessment and physical therapy referral
contraindicated_actions: Escalating levodopa indefinitely without reassessing diagnosis
Prescribing dopamine agonists (risk of hallucinations/impulse control without benefit)
key_reasoning_points: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (especially downgaze) is the hallmark of PSP-Richardson syndrome
Oculocephalic reflex preserving full vertical range confirms the palsy is supranuclear (brainstem intact)
Early backward falls within the first year distinguish PSP from PD (PD falls are typically later)
Axial rigidity predominating over limb rigidity is characteristic of PSP vs PD
Erect posture with retrocollis is the opposite of PD's stooped posture
Poor levodopa response (<30%) is expected in PSP and should trigger reconsideration of PD diagnosis
Hummingbird sign on MRI reflects selective midbrain atrophy (midbrain:pons ratio <0.52)
Frontal-executive cognitive profile with preserved memory differs from AD but aligns with subcortical-frontal tauopathy
Symmetric DaTscan loss differs from PD's typically asymmetric pattern

red_herrings.correct_interpretation: Parkinsonism with early falls should trigger consideration of PSP from the start
DaTscan is abnormal in any neurodegenerative parkinsonism including PSP — does not distinguish PSP from PD
Was started empirically but patient had poor response — this is a key red flag for atypical parkinsonism

metadata.diagnostic_challenge: Differentiating progressive supranuclear palsy from Parkinson disease in a patient referred with 'atypical PD' after poor levodopa response, requiring recognition of vertical gaze palsy, early falls, and characteristic imaging
metadata.educational_value: Teaches recognition of PSP-Richardson syndrome cardinal features including vertical supranuclear gaze palsy, early backward falls, axial-predominant rigidity, poor levodopa response, and the hummingbird sign on MRI
teaching_points: Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (especially downgaze) is the hallmark of PSP-Richardson syndrome
Early backward falls within the first 18 months should trigger consideration of PSP over PD
The oculocephalic reflex distinguishes supranuclear from nuclear gaze palsy
Hummingbird sign and morning glory sign on MRI are characteristic of PSP
Poor levodopa response at adequate doses should prompt reconsideration of PD diagnosis
DaTscan is abnormal in PSP as well as PD — it does not distinguish between them
Dysphagia with aspiration is a major morbidity and mortality concern in PSP
